metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC3: Inline the full proving command (the exact `bash` invocation with stub PATH, the canary-file assertion, and the expected output check) directly in the plan, and define what `verify_commands` is (path, format, how it is consulted) so the command is reproducible without external knowledge.
findings.finding: AC3's proving command is described in prose but its actual invocation is deferred to an external artifact (`verify_commands`) that is nowhere defined in the plan — an executor cannot locate, reproduce, or validate this command from the plan text alone.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace 'Proving command recorded in verify_commands' with the literal command, e.g.:
```
mkdir -p /tmp/stubs && for b in docker dnf systemctl; do printf '#!/bin/sh\ntouch /tmp/canary_%s\n' "$b" > /tmp/stubs/$b && chmod +x /tmp/stubs/$b; done
out=$(PATH=/tmp/stubs:$PATH bash infra/scripts/compose-up.sh --print-volumes)
[[ $? -eq 0 ]]
echo "$out" | grep -qx 'gerrit_reviewbot_tickets'
! ls /tmp/canary_* 2>/dev/null
```
If `verify_commands` is a repo file (e.g. `infra/scripts/verify_commands.sh`), define its path and state that the above command is appended there.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or embed a self-test in AC3) that runs `compose-up.sh` in no-arg mode with stub `docker` capturing all `docker volume create` invocations and asserts the expected set of volume names is created, AND document a rollback path (e.g., revert commit on the stacked change) if the refactored provisioning loop is found broken post-land.
findings.finding: The plan refactors compose-up.sh's existing provisioning loops (hoisting the subdir list into SITE_SUBDIRS and routing both loops through a shared helper function), changing logic that production `compose up` invocations rely on. No AC tests the normal zero-arg boot/provisioning path after the refactoring, and no rollback plan is described for the case where the helper-function refactoring breaks production provisioning.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3: in addition to the --print-volumes canary test, run the script in normal mode with a stub `docker` that logs its arguments to a file, then assert the logged `docker volume create` calls match the expected volume set. Add a one-line rollback note: 'If the refactored loop is found broken post-land, revert this change; e991 is independent and its revert is not required.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit regression note or test coverage statement for the existing 8 provisioned volumes after the SITE_SUBDIRS refactor — e.g., extend AC3 to assert that the helper function produces correct volume names for all entries including pre-existing ones, not just gerrit_reviewbot_tickets.
findings.finding: The plan's refactor of compose-up.sh's two existing provisioning loops (replacing two hardcoded `for d in git index cache db etc logs plugins reviewbot` loops with a shared SITE_SUBDIRS variable and helper function) is an in-scope behavioral modification to production volume-provisioning logic, but the plan names no regression guard specifically for the existing 8 volumes — only AC3 checks the full print output (including gerrit_reviewbot_tickets), not that the actual docker volume create calls for all pre-existing volumes remain structurally correct.
findings.location: What (mechanism) section — 'infra/scripts/compose-up.sh: hoist the currently-duplicated hardcoded subdir list' paragraph; AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit equivalence assertion: include a table or test (e.g. a third AC3-style self-test or a comment in compose-up.sh) that maps each of the 8 existing subdirs through the new helper and asserts the derived name matches the known pre-existing volume name, so a derivation-rule change is caught before it reaches production.
findings.finding: The plan refactors compose-up.sh by replacing hardcoded volume name literals with a derived form (`gerrit_` + subdir with `-` → `_` via a new shared helper). It does not explicitly assert that the helper's output is byte-identical to every one of the 8 pre-existing hardcoded names. If any derivation produces a different string than the old literal, compose-up.sh will silently create a new empty volume instead of reattaching to the existing data-bearing one — a data-loss scenario on the next production `compose up`.
findings.location: ## What (mechanism) — compose-up.sh paragraph: 'hoist the currently-duplicated hardcoded subdir list into ONE top-level variable … with volume-name derivation (`gerrit_` + subdir with `-` → `_`) in one shared helper function'
findings.suggested_fix: In compose-up.sh, add a static assertion block immediately after the helper definition — a `case` statement or array that pairs each subdir with its expected volume name and `diff`s the helper output against the expected literal. Alternatively, extend AC3 to assert that `--print-volumes` output for the 8 existing subdirs exactly matches the hardcoded list from the last known-good state.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR or CLAUDE.md entry documenting the `--print-volumes` introspection convention (why it exists, what consumes it, and the rejected textual-grep alternative) so the cross-component contract survives beyond the commit message.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern — a bash script exposing a `--print-volumes` introspection mode consumed by a CI gate check — that crosses a component boundary (compose-up.sh → config-check.sh) and establishes a new interface contract. Neither an ADR nor a CLAUDE.md/design-doc update is mentioned to capture this decision for future maintainers who may not know why the mode exists or that removing/renaming it breaks the gate.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `docs/adr/NNNN-compose-up-print-volumes-introspection.md` (or a section in CLAUDE.md) with: decision = expose `--print-volumes` for machine-readable CI consumption; alternatives considered = grep/awk (rejected — same drift failure class); consumers = config-check.sh check 5. Link it from both script headers alongside the existing contract note.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm or rebut: with only one call-site (config-check.sh check 5), does the `--print-volumes` inter-script API meet the Rule-of-Three bar, or could check 5 source the volume list without a CLI mode (e.g., sourcing just the SITE_SUBDIRS variable declaration in a subshell, or extracting it from a standalone data file)?
findings.finding: The `--print-volumes` mode is a new inter-script CLI abstraction with exactly one planned call-site (config-check.sh check 5), violating Rule-of-Three. The plan's own justification ('single consumer is acceptable: the mode is 4 lines') addresses implementation cost but not the design heuristic: adding a machine-readable argument-dispatch mode to a side-effecting provisioning script creates a cross-script contract that must be maintained even if check 5 is later removed or rewritten. An alternative the plan explicitly considered and rejected (textual parsing) is already partially available: since check 5 already uses python3+yaml.safe_load on the compose file to extract external volume names, it could equally read the SITE_SUBDIRS assignment from compose-up.sh via a sourced subshell (sourcing only through the variable declaration block) or embed the list as a standalone data file — keeping the derivation in one place without adding a CLI mode.
findings.location: What (mechanism) section — `--print-volumes` mode; Alternative considered section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name `infra/scripts/config-check.sh` explicitly as a primary edit target in the 'What' section (parallel bullet to the compose-up.sh bullet), describing the addition of check 5 and its two embedded self-tests.
findings.finding: infra/scripts/config-check.sh is a concrete required edit target (the plan describes adding check 5 plus two embedded self-tests to it) but is never explicitly named as an edit target in the plan's 'What' section — it appears only as an inline reference ('config-check.sh check 5'). An implementor reading the What section for their file checklist will not find it listed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a top-level bullet: '`infra/scripts/config-check.sh`: add check 5 — extract external volumes from docker-compose.yml via python3/yaml.safe_load, run compose-up.sh --print-volumes, compare sets; embed AC1 and AC4 self-tests that auto-run on every make config-check invocation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief note in the 'Alternative considered' section explaining why a shared data file (e.g., a sourced `volumes.env` listing SITE_SUBDIRS, imported by both compose-up.sh and config-check.sh) was considered and rejected in favour of the `--print-volumes` CLI contract, or adopt that simpler pattern if no strong reason against it exists.
findings.finding: The plan considers only one alternative (grepping compose-up.sh textually) before committing to the `--print-volumes` inter-script API pattern, without evaluating the established prior-art approach of a shared data file (e.g., a sourced `volumes.env` or JSON manifest) as the single source of truth for both compose-up.sh and config-check.sh. This shared-config pattern is widely used in deployment toolchains (Ansible group_vars, Kubernetes kustomize overlays, Terraform locals) precisely to avoid coupling a boot orchestrator's CLI contract to a CI gate script.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
